name: Lina Bo Bardi
bio: Lina Bo Bardi was an Italian-born Brazilian architect who fused modernist rigor with the warmth, color, and craft traditions of Brazil. Her buildings reject cold monumentality in favor of spaces that embrace community, improvisation, and everyday life — concrete structures made alive by vivid reds, yellows, and the human activity they frame.

name: SANAA
bio: SANAA, founded by Kazuyo Sejima and Ryue Nishizawa, is a Japanese architectural firm celebrated for its radically transparent, thin-skinned structures that dissolve the boundary between inside and outside. Winners of the 2010 Pritzker Prize, their work embodies lightness, openness, and an almost ethereal relationship with landscape and light.

portrait.alt: Portrait of SANAA — Kazuyo Sejima and Ryue Nishizawa

name: Álvaro Siza Vieira
bio: Álvaro Siza Vieira is a Portuguese architect whose poetic modernism transforms simple materials — white plaster, concrete, marble — into buildings of quiet, luminous power. Winner of the 1992 Pritzker Prize, his work is defined by an exquisite sensitivity to site, light, and the subtle dialogue between architecture and landscape.

name: John Pawson
bio: John Pawson is a British architect and designer whose work pursues the philosophy of 'minimum' — the point where reduction ceases to diminish meaning and instead amplifies it. His spaces are defined by precise proportions, the play of natural light, and an almost monastic devotion to visual and spatial clarity.

name: Tadao Ando
bio: Tadao Ando is a self-taught Japanese architect who transforms raw concrete into spaces of profound spiritual power. His buildings orchestrate light, water, and geometry with such precision that they become meditative experiences — austere surfaces that paradoxically feel warm and deeply human.

name: Zaha Hadid
bio: Zaha Hadid shattered conventions with fluid, gravity-defying forms that reimagined what buildings could be. The first woman to receive the Pritzker Prize, her parametric designs — swooping curves, fractured geometries, impossible cantilevers — brought radical dynamism to contemporary architecture.

name: Peter Zumthor
bio: Peter Zumthor crafts buildings with the care of a master carpenter, attending to every material, joint, and atmospheric quality. His architecture is about presence — the weight of stone, the warmth of wood, the way light enters a room — creating spaces that engage all the senses.

name: Herzog & de Meuron
bio: Jacques Herzog and Pierre de Meuron transform the surface of architecture into an expressive medium — perforated copper, etched glass, woven bamboo. Pritzker Prize winners in 2001, their buildings range from intimate wineries to Olympic stadiums, always pushing material innovation.

name: Renzo Piano
bio: Renzo Piano combines engineering ingenuity with humanistic sensibility, creating buildings that feel both technologically adventurous and warm. From the radical Centre Pompidou to the delicate Shard, his work spans five decades of reinventing what public architecture can be.

name: Le Corbusier
bio: Le Corbusier revolutionized modern architecture with his Five Points — pilotis, free plan, free façade, horizontal windows, and roof garden. His vision of architecture as a 'machine for living' shaped the 20th century, from the white villas of Poissy to the raw concrete of Chandigarh.
